content.rendered:
Discussion Topic

1.Post the link to the Youtube video of yourself teaching a 4 to 10-minute lesson based on your lesson plan. Follow the guidelines and instructions posted after Week 8 Module.

2. View three of your classmates’ videos and  comment on their teaching a lesson. In your comments be guided by what you’ve learned earlier by observing, planning and teaching a lesson. Make sure to start with two “glows” (things that stood out) and one “grow” (one thing that may be developed further). You can also go back to the six TESOL  Principles that you used in lesson observation, but don’t forget that what you watched was just  a snippet of a lesson. 

For this assignment you are going to work in groups of  3-4  so that everyone can get feedback about their teaching. Once you’ve commented on the lessons in your  group, you are welcome to watch and comment on  the lessons outside your group. Make sure to post in a timely manner – your classmates depend on you.

Discussion Thread

Tina Liu

Mar 8, 2021 9:37pm


Thank you Professor Leijko-Lacan and hi Colleen, SungEun and Gabriela, 

,

Here’s my video shot in the best VR classroom my husband has made to date.  It is located in a sea of floating islands in the sky, for a lesson on five prepositions words.  I didn’t see the time limit on the assignment instructions, so I took advantage of that to show a fuller lesson snippet of 20 mins still, seeing the sample provided.  Feel free to fast forward to each section with title screens: Ms Tina’s Magical Island of Prepositions 


I must go through one more final edit to correct a few of my own grammatical bloopers to really pass for a native blond and blue-eyed Caucasian, before showing this video to recruiters.  I chose to make my avatar as a blonde and blue-eyed Caucasian, to address the teacher preferential hiring practice for them in Taiwan, a job search challenge I will be facing but also a business opportunity to target, when I’ll move there in two or three months.  Here’s a bit more about it in a demo video, the lesson plan, and a paper I did for the Culture course that gives more background on this.


If any of you have had classes with fellow classmate Rosie Bu, you’ll hear her voice through one of the student avatars, because she volunteers as one of the actors of my shoot.  I hope you’ll like it!


The thumbnail of my video is not showing, so I’ve embedded an image here as a sneak preview:


Colleen Laurent
Mar 10, 2021 10:40am


Great job on the visuals! It’s definitely a fun and unique approach to teaching. I especially like that you can demonstrate words like on/at/across/in with the avatar in ways that you wouldn’t be able to in a regular classroom.I like the way you incorporated a lot of repetition and detailed explanations.I thought this was a great lesson and it’s interesting to see how VR can be used as a teaching tool. I’m wondering about the frequency of using VR, and whether it would just be an occasional enhancement tool or if it would be the main teaching avenue. Perhaps this is an age issue, but I find it harder to stay engaged with educational animations. There’s something about not being able to connect with a real face that seems to lower my concentration after a while. My guess is that perhaps this wouldn’t be the case for younger people who have grown up with more video games and technology, but it might be a consideration for older students.


Tina Liu
Mar 14, 2021 5:56pm


Hi Colleen,
 
Thank you for the compliments! I hope to have your permission to publish it as testimonials on my eventual company website to create and sell timeshares of our VR classrooms. 
 
It’s interesting that you mention age, as I find it almost impossible to get my husband, age 49 out of VR, as it is so engaging. It’s a fundamentally different thing to have one way interaction like watching an animation vs the two-way interaction in VR. The phrase ‘it takes two to tango’ comes to mind in this situation, or possibly now we change it to ‘it takes many to tango’ for this very unique and moving community experience.
 
In fact, his VR community that he is doing other projects with, prefer only to meet VR space, even just to have a chat, rather than do teleconferencing or calls. I personally I’m 52, not a gamer, like watching only exceptional animation films and have found it hard not to get addicted to it, once spending three hours straight in there without realizing it.  The danger of VR is actually the reverse of a boring experience, it’s often considered better than reality, for people who spend a ton of time in VR find it hard to return to reality, because of the stark difference. VR is also effective for different kinds of therapy.
 
I would suggest you and anyone else in this group who has not experienced social VR, try it, so you can truly understand the scope of what my classrooms are about. My husband and I were both kind of skeptical on how this type of platform could have this degree of depth, and have become believers experiencing it especially in 3-D with the goggles headsets and creating just one art project  only six months ago and now we are forming a new business on it. 
 
You see the world you are in through a first person view. You have two controllers, one goes in each hand, and it uses motion tracking to know where they are. This means when you look for your hands, they are wherever they are in the physical reality and the finger motions articulate in real time as you move your fingers… It’s like you are really there… You then see another avatar, and hear their voice, now they are really there. 
 
The next very simple thing is truly what makes this experience so unique, you can put your arms around them and give them a hug, without fear.  The human connection factor, we have lost, especially due to Covid-19 has been reimagined in VR. This has unearthed another issue that has also been overlooked, before Covid-19, there was still a certain percentage of people who cannot have this human connection to begin with due to health or mobility challenges and VR has brought the world to them. This is why VR is so important. 
 
I would like to end on a more comical note, I invite you all to come to this environment in Altspace where three grandmothers created a backyard just to throw all manner of garbage at our formal former president’s face.  I’d be happy to be the tour guide and help everyone get in there. Perhaps we can have a class field trip.  Please feel free to connect with me on LinkedIn or Facebook to stay in touch, the profiles are on my bio page here on Canvas. 
 
With gratitude,
 
Tina


SungEun Lee (She/Her/Hers)
Mar 13, 2021 12:10pm


Hi Tina,


Wow, you and your husband are really talented! This is a brilliant and unique idea! Your lesson has awesome visual supports and I like how you reacted to your students’ responses to encourage them. I also like how you planned your lesson as student-centered learning environment. Your explanation was very clear because you used many hand gestures and talked at the appropriate pace. Your explanation may be more effective if you present images of waterfall, an umbrella, a cup and stuff when you mention such words to explain each preposition. In that way, the students would have better understanding if they are at the beginning level. But overall, I loved your lesson! That was so cool! 


Tina Liu
Mar 14, 2021 2:35pm


Thank you for your generous compliments and helpful suggestions. The waterfall was actually illustrated in 3-D VR Realia,. The other words, items or places I used came unplanned during my lesson and we do not yet have the capability of instantly generating objects unplanned. So I used gestures instead. Once the application my husband is developing to generate objects, letters and words instantly in our classrooms, is ready, then I will be able to do so, more readily.


I had meant to ask you if Korea has the same preferential hiring practices for non-east Asian teachers as we do in Taiwan? The VR classrooms teaching solution will work for you too. I’d love to show you how it works, and give you access to teach in our classrooms and have your collaboration on accounting help on register our global startup in a fiscally advantageous location in Asia.


By the way, I hope to have your permission to publish your kindest compliments on my company website as a testimonial. Please let me know if you have any objections. You will be able to see it soon on www.VRealTime.net


Please feel free to connect with me on LinkedIn or Facebook to stay in touch. My profiles are on my bio page here in Canvas.
Much gratitude in advance!
Tina


SungEun Lee (She/Her/Hers)
Mar 15, 2021 1:24am


Hi Tina,


Thank you for your explanation. I honestly did not have anything to say regarding the “grow” point since you made a fabulous lesson,
but I just had to find something LOL
I just added you on my FB. Please PM me on FB then I will let you know about the hiring practices and salary gaps of different cities in South Korea. I would love to get to know about the VR classroom teaching solution as well! and yes, you can use my comment on your website Thank you! 


Tina Liu
Mar 15, 2021 9:17pm


Thank you SungEun!  I look forward to taking you on a tour of Altspace next week!  I’m glad we’re connected on FB now


Patricia Steele
Mar 13, 2021 12:12pm


Dear Tina,


Hello! Our team has only three members, so I hope you don’t mind that I am popping in here to see your lesson. I absolutely love how you interact with the students in this world. Your students get fun, positive feedback and encouragement. Your visuals are great! You created real-world importance at the beginning by stating that the students need to use prepositions so you can find each other. Perhaps you can increase the rigor? Maybe you can tie in a short story that develops at each station. Or you might include non-examples or likely misunderstandings. I was thinking about how to include higher order thinking skills. I wondered about asking students to experiment with each type of noun to see if they can generalize and extend their knowledge. For example, under the tree (thing), under the giraffe (animal/person), under the meadow (place), under 80% (idea). Good luck in Taiwan! You definitely sound like a native English speaker!


Tina Liu
Mar 14, 2021 4:42pm


Dear Patricia,
 
I’m so grateful you chose my video to see and offering me your most valuable advice a second time in this course. Especially since my video is double the length compared to the others, it’s also, for this reason, I could not include any more situational examples making this possibly one-hour lesson even longer.
 
The other words not illustrated graphically were items or places I mentioned unplanned during my lesson and we do not yet have the capability of instantly generating objects, or words unplanned. So I used gestures instead. Once the application my husband is developing to generate objects, letters and words instantly in our classrooms, is finished, then I will be able to do so, more readily.
 
Your suggestion of the different types of prepositions and examples, as well as higher-level thinking I thought about too are great. I will certainly implement them into this lesson for the future.
 
Thank you for affirming that I can pass for a native speaker, you might have noticed many English Grammar bloopers I made that needs editing out Ha ha ha… I am thankful for your vote of confidence for sure!
 
I hope to have your permission to publish your kindest compliments on my company website as a testimonial.  Please let me know if you have any objections. You will be able to see it soon on www.VRealTime.net 
 Please also feel free to connect with me on LinkedIn or Facebook to stay in touch. My profiles are on my bio page here in Canvas.
 
Much gratitude!
 
Tina


Gabriela Miranda Laguna (She/Her/Hers)
Mar 13, 2021 9:37pm


Hello Tina, 


Your videos are fascinating. Teaching virtually comes with its own set of challenges especially during a pandemic, and you and your idea about videos with 3-D models are amazing. These videos will be awesome for your future students. It definitely, increases attention from the students because it is easier to comprehend and captures more attention than text or static images.


I recommend you to speak with more energy and changing the tones. Maybe it would be good if your face appeared in some shots.


Tina Liu
Mar 14, 2021 4:16pm


Hi Gabriela,

I deeply appreciate your encouraging compliments, comments, and suggestions, which helped me understand there may be some misunderstanding to these videos that appeared like animated cartoons. I replied to your similar comment on my Methods class video too but maybe you did not see it.


My videos are filmed in live and completely immersive VR environments. Each student participates in a first-person view as though they are in front of an instructor in physical reality. The ‘video’ was shot in this way to demonstrate how an actual class will operate. These videos are not cartoon animations for students to watch, they are 3D graphic representations of classroom environments, teaching materials, and of the people in them, live and in real-time.  So in essence, the students do not watch a recorded video but participate in a live 3D VR environment, which is available on VR goggles, like Oculus Quest as well as in 2D on a standard computer, both Windows and Mac.  

 

My husband is developing applications that will allow the instructor to instantaneously produce ‘in-world any 3D object that is contained in our library of teaching objects. This app will insert words and objects that match them, for example, the word ‘Apple’ right next to a 3D apple, which the teacher and students will be able to interact with, including moving them around as they would in a video game. He is also planning on creating an entire set of Cuisenaire rods, with the words already printed on them. It will also enable the students to have pens to write within the VR classroom. Tilt Brush is an example of this. 


On showing my face, I purposely try to avoid revealing my ethnic identity, addressing preferential hiring practices in Taiwan and Mainland China for non-East Asian teachers. This is why I chose a Caucasian-looking avatar to represent me. And since the students are in the VR environments with me, and not watching a Zoom chat window or video, unless if I enable a video screen inside of my VR classroom for students to see my real face, they would anyway only be able to see the lower part of my face since my eyes would be covered by the VR goggles headset.


VR has given us a whole new level of possibilities for remote teaching, including being able to teach students globally in fantastical worlds of imagination, where anything could be made possible, fully immersive and interactive without losing social contact, rather than just the one-way video conference type of remote learning. Any teacher will be able to come into our classrooms with their goggles or computer and teach their students with the material we provide or custom make for their class. 


I hope you will all join me in understanding VR environments better.  Please feel free to connect with me on LinkedIn or Facebook, so I can give you a virtual tour of these classrooms to experience and know them for yourself. My contact information is on my is bio page here on Canvas.


By the way, I hope to have your permission to publish your kindest compliments on my company website as a testimonial. Please let me know if you have any objections. You will be able to see it soon on www.VRealTime.net

Much gratitude in advance!


Cheers… Tina


Gabriela Miranda Laguna (She/Her/Hers)
Mar 17, 2021 2:31pm


Thank you very much for your kind and complete explanation about VR. I will see on www.VRealTime.net. 

or sure.


I didn’t know about the importance to avoid an Asian face in China, and about hiring practices for teachers. Neither about the importance to choose a Caucasian-looking. Thank you very much for your information.
Of course you have my permission to publish my comments!
Great job Tina, I hope to see you someday.


Tina Liu

Mar 17, 2021 9:28pm


Thank you Gabriela!  Please join our field trip to AltspaceVR!  I sent an email about it to your inbox.  I look forward to connecting with you and staying in touch.  Count me in as a friend in Taiwan if you will ever travel there!


Eddie Murray

Mar 14, 2021 12:20am


Hi Tina, Awesome video lesson! Thanks for sharing.


Tina Liu
Mar 14, 2021 10:18pm


Thank you Eddie, I’m getting permission to organize a field trip into AltspaceVR.  Join us if you can!  Connect with me on LinkedIn or FB to stay in touch, my profile is on my Bio page here on Canvas,


Viktorija Lejko-Lacan
Mar 16, 2021 8:53pm


Tina, A very different way of teaching and an exciting way of learning, particularly for students who already are into gaming. The visual experience is wonderful . The objective of the lesson is clear – learn prepositions , so you’ll be able to find someone. Maybe the next step could be developing a story about someone who’s lost.


Tina Liu

Mar 17, 2021  9:30pm


Yes, indeed Professor Lejko-Lacan!  I’m glad you enjoyed it and thank you for the encouragement and the great idea of the story of someone getting lost.  The treasure hunt was a bit about that… to find and be found
